ema.author: European Medicines Agency (HMPC)
ema.title: European Union herbal monograph on Verbena officinalis L., herba (Verbenae herba)

review2020.title: Verbena officinalis (Common Vervain) - A Review on the Investigations of This Medicinally Important Plant Species

actions.action: Nervine / anxiolytic for nervous tension and mild anxiety; supports sleep (traditional)
Digestive bitter and relief of mild mental stress

constituents.name: Iridoid glycosides (verbenalin, hastatoside)
constituents.note: Characteristic constituents; verbenalin has a dopaminergic effect.

constituents.name: Flavonoids, phenolic acids and essential oil

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated in traditional use. Avoid during pregnancy - vervain has a traditional uterine-stimulant (oxytocic) reputation linked to verbenalin.
May add to the effect of sedatives; use in breastfeeding is not recommended in the absence of data.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from regulatory and scientific literature (EMA HMPC monograph for Verbenae herba; Planta Medica review). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (anxiety, insomnia, nervous-tension, inflammation, depression); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

herbalreality.author: Herbal Reality
herbalreality.title: Wild Cherry (Prunus serotina): Benefits, Uses, Research

theherbalmedic.author: The Herbal Medic
theherbalmedic.title: Wild Cherry Bark (Prunus serotina) - Soothing Herb for Coughs & Lungs

actions.action: Antitussive - calms a persistent dry, irritating cough (prunasin quietens the cough reflex)
Antispasmodic for bronchial cough and bronchitis (eases spasm of the airways)
Soothes sore throat and respiratory irritation (traditional)

constituents.name: Cyanogenic glycoside prunasin
constituents.note: Releases trace hydrogen cyanide that calms the cough reflex - the basis of both the antitussive action and the safety caution.

contraindications.note: Contains cyanogenic compounds: use only properly dried, prepared bark and avoid large or prolonged doses (fresh bark has a higher toxic content). It suppresses the cough reflex, so it suits dry, unproductive coughs - not productive coughs where mucus needs clearing.
Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding because of the cyanogenic glycosides.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from general phytotherapy literature (Herbal Reality and The Herbal Medic monographs). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (cough, bronchitis, sore-throat); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

herbalreality.author: Herbal Reality
herbalreality.title: Wild Lettuce (Lactuca virosa): Benefits, Uses, Safety

rxlist.author: RxList
rxlist.title: Wild Lettuce: Health Benefits, Side Effects, Uses, Dose & Precautions

actions.action: Mild analgesic / anodyne for pain (lactucin and lactucopicrin act on the central nervous system)
Calming for nervous tension and restlessness
Antitussive for an irritable, dry cough (traditional)

constituents.name: Sesquiterpene lactones (lactucin, lactucopicrin)
constituents.note: Bitter, centrally-active constituents responsible for the sedative and pain-relieving effects.

constituents.name: Lactucarium (the dried milky latex)
constituents.note: The historical medicinal preparation, concentrating the active lactones.

contraindications.note: Can be unsafe in large amounts or if harvested too early: overdose can cause sweating, fast heartbeat, dilated pupils, dizziness, ringing in the ears, vision changes, heavy sedation, breathing difficulty and, in extreme cases, death.

Its sedative effect can add to that of sedatives and alcohol - avoid combining and do not use before driving. As a daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant it can cause allergy; avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from general literature (Herbal Reality and RxList monographs). Record status set to needs-review because of the overdose toxicity risk and limited modern evidence. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (insomnia, pain, nervous-tension, cough); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

lima2013.author: Lima, C.M., Lima, A.K., Melo, M.G.D., Serafini, M.R., Oliveira, D.L., de Almeida, E.B., Barreto, R.S.S., Nogueira, P.C., Moraes, V.R.S., Oliveira, E.R.A., de Albuquerque, R.L.C., Quintans-Junior, L.J. and Araujo, A.A.S.
lima2013.title: Bioassay-guided evaluation of Dioscorea villosa - an acute and subchronic toxicity, antinociceptive and anti-inflammatory approach

actions.action: Antispasmodic / analgesic for menstrual cramps and muscle spasm (antinociceptive in animal models)
Anti-inflammatory for joint and inflammatory pain
Traditional support for menopausal symptoms (see safety note - it does NOT act as a natural progesterone)

constituents.name: Steroidal saponins yielding diosgenin
constituents.note: Diosgenin is an industrial precursor for making steroid hormones in the laboratory, but it is NOT converted into hormones in the human body.

contraindications.note: IMPORTANT myth-bust: wild yam / diosgenin is NOT converted into progesterone or DHEA in the body, so it does not work as a 'natural progesterone' - claims to that effect are incorrect.

Generally well tolerated orally (no acute or subchronic toxicity in animal studies); large doses may cause nausea or vomiting. Avoid medicinal doses in pregnancy and breastfeeding and in hormone-sensitive conditions as a precaution.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine bioassay/toxicity study). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (menstrual-cramps, menopause, muscle-spasm); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

vlachojannis2009.title: A systematic review on the effectiveness of willow bark for musculoskeletal pain

ema.author: European Medicines Agency (HMPC)
ema.title: European Union herbal monograph on Salix [various species], cortex (Salicis cortex)

actions.action: Analgesic and anti-inflammatory for low back pain (moderate evidence; dose-dependent, around 240 mg salicin/day)
Relief of joint pain, osteoarthritis and rheumatic complaints

constituents.name: Salicin and related salicylates
constituents.note: Metabolised in the body to salicylic acid - the 'natural aspirin' that gives the analgesic, anti-inflammatory and antipyretic effects.

constituents.name: Polyphenols, flavonoids and tannins
constituents.note: Contribute additionally to the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity.

contraindications.note: Because it works like aspirin, avoid willow bark if you are allergic to aspirin or salicylates, and do NOT give it to children or teenagers with a viral illness (risk of Reye's syndrome). Use caution in asthma (salicylate sensitivity).
Avoid with anticoagulant/antiplatelet medicines (bleeding risk) and in active peptic ulcer; avoid in pregnancy (especially the third trimester) and breastfeeding.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and regulatory literature (Phytotherapy Research systematic review; EMA HMPC monograph for Salicis cortex).
